column2: Art From The Heart T-Shirt - Bauhaus Colours
column3:
This 'Art From The Heart' t-shirt in the Bauhaus colourway is a tribute to Modernism. I have chosen the 'Ochre 'coloured t-shirt for this design to give it a grounded, industrial-yet-natural foundation that feels both vintage and timeless.

The design uses the iconic Modernist palette - the primary triad of Terracotta Red, Mustard, and Blues. Named after the influential German design school, this palette is known in the art world for its bold, functional beauty and its belief that art and design should be unified into one powerful expression.

Whether it’s a thoughtful gift for the artist in your life or a well-deserved treat for yourself, this piece is designed for those who find beauty in the process, the palette, and the history of the craft.
column5: Apparel & Accessories > Clothing > Clothing Tops > T-Shirts

column2: Art From The Heart T-Shirt Monet Colours
column3:
This 'Art From The Heart' t-shirt in the Monet colourway is a tribute to the Impressionist movement. I have chosen the 'Aloe' coloured t-shirt for this design to evoke the soft, tranquil greens of the Giverny gardens and the reflection of light on water.

The design uses a palette inspired by Monet’s Water Lilies - a harmonious blend of dusty pinks, soft lavenders and mossy greens. This selection is well known with artists for capturing the fleeting nature of light and atmosphere, moving away from harsh lines to embrace a world of soft, evocative colour.

Whether it’s a thoughtful gift for the artist in your life or a well-deserved treat for yourself, this piece is designed for those who find beauty in the process, the palette, and the history of the craft.
column5: Apparel & Accessories > Clothing > Clothing Tops > T-Shirts

column34: Front

column2: Art From The Heart T-Shirt - Van Gogh Colours
column3:
This 'Art From The Heart' t-shirt in the Van Gogh colourway is a tribute to Post-Impressionism. I have chosen the 'Mindful Blue' coloured t-shirt for this design to provide a deep, contemplative backdrop that mimics a swirling twilight sky.

The design uses a high-contrast palette of vibrant oranges and deep blues, inspired by the complementary colours Van Gogh used to create emotional intensity. This combination is legendary in the art world for its raw energy and its ability to make the "heart" of the painting practically vibrate with life against the darkness.

Whether it’s a thoughtful gift for the artist in your life or a well-deserved treat for yourself, this piece is designed for those who find beauty in the process, the palette, and the history of the craft.
column5: Apparel & Accessories > Clothing > Clothing Tops > T-Shirts

column2: Art From The Heart T-Shirt - Zorn Palette Colours
column3:
This 'Art From The Heart' t-shirt in the Zorn Palette colourway is a tribute to the Old Masters. I have chosen the 'Honey Paper' coloured t-shirt for this design to mimic vintage parchment.

The design uses the celebrated Zorn Palette—a restricted selection of just four pigments (Yellow Ochre, Vermilion, Ivory Black, and White). Named after the Swedish painter Anders Zorn, this palette is legendary in the art world for its ability to create a natural, sophisticated harmony, especially useful in portraiture.

Whether it’s a thoughtful gift for the artist in your life or a well-deserved treat for yourself, this piece is designed for those who find beauty in the process, the palette, and the history of the craft.

column2: Wuthering Heights Travel Poster - Top Withens, Haworth Art Print
column3:
Capture the Atmosphere of the Yorkshire Moors - Wuthering Heights Gift Idea

Capture the wild, atmospheric beauty of the Yorkshire Moors with this vibrant Wuthering Heights travel poster.

Inspired by the haunting landscapes that fueled Emily Brontë’s imagination, this art print features the iconic Top Withens, the ruined farmhouse near Haworth widely believed to be the inspiration for the Earnshaw home in Wuthering Heights.

Why This Print Belongs in Your Collection

Whether you are a lifelong literature lover or a hiker who has braved the climb from Haworth Main Street up to the windswept moors, this piece serves as a permanent portal to that landscape. I wanted to capture the tumultous energy of the moors, the shifting light, the dramatic clouds, and the sense of isolation that makes the Brontë sisters' work so timeless.

Expertly Crafted for Thoughtful Collectors

As with all my work, this Haworth-inspired art print is designed to bring instant character and a pop of sophisticated colour to your home.

  • Signature Style: A modern, vibrant twist on the classic vintage travel poster aesthetic.

  • Hand-Signed: Each print is hand-signed by me (Georgina Westley) in pencil, ensuring you receive a piece of art that feels personal and authentic.

  • Gallery Quality: I use archival matte 230gsm paper and non-fade pigment inks (Epson SureColour) to ensure your Yorkshire landscape stays as vivid as the day it arrives.

The Perfect Gift for Brontë Fans

Searching for a unique Wuthering Heights gift? This poster is a thoughtful choice for birthdays, anniversaries, or housewarmings. It’s more than just wall decor; it’s a celebration of literary history and the rugged beauty of the Pennines.

Easy to Frame

To make your life easier, I’ve designed this print in standard A4, A3, and A2 sizes. This means you won’t need custom framing; it will drop perfectly into any standard frame from your favorite local shop.

column2: Hamnet III: The Grief That Does Not Speak
column3:
Original Hamnet-Inspired Abstract Painting

Description: Inspired by the film Hamnet. You can read all about my process over on my blog.

Palette: Earthy ochre, soft blues, and emerging rust, crimson spots and leafy greens.

Technique: Layered washes and sharp, linear marks.

Size: 6" x 6" painting in a 12" mount - easy to frame.

Authentication: Signed. Includes a signed Certificate of Authenticity.

Price: £60

Each of the three paintings in this series is a unique, one-of-a-kind original. While they share a palette and a story, no two are the same, reflecting the individual ghosts and memories revealed during the process.

column3:
The ultimate creative self-care day awaits. Ditch the passwords and pick up a paintbrush in this 6-hour immersive workshop led by me, Georgina Westley. We will be playing with colour and composition to gently spark your curiosity and guide you through creative blocks. Find or reconnect with your inner artist and embrace the joy of making something just for you.

Date: Sunday, November 23rd, 2025

Time: 10:00 am - 4:00 pm 

Location: Tempsford Memorial Hall, Church St, Tempsford, Sandy SG19 2AW

Includes:

  • All art materials provided - just show up ready to create.
  • Expert guidance from me in a calm, encouraging environment.
  • Take home your mounted piece of finished artwork ready to display.
  • Free-flowing tea and coffee throughout the day.
  • Delicious homemade cake for that essential creative fuel.

Please note:

  • Please bring your own packed lunch for the day.
  • Bring an apron/old shirt or wear clothes you don't mind getting a splash of paint on.

Upon purchase, you'll receive a PDF ticket via email that serves as your entry pass.

Please present your ticket (digital or printed) on the day.

Cancellation policy:

My class sizes are small to guarantee a high-value experience. I purchase materials and secure the hall well in advance, meaning last-minute cancellations make it difficult to cover these committed costs.

  • Transfers: if you can no longer attend, you may transfer your ticket to a friend or family member at any time. Just let me know the new participant's name via email.
  • Refunds (more than 14 days notice): I can offer a full refund.
  • Refunds (less than 14 days notice): no refunds are available due to materials and venue costs already committed. You are very welcome to use the ticket transfer option.

column3:
Inspired by the transformative power of light, I created this series of five paintings which I have called Alpine Sunrise. The overriding feeling I had when painting these at my kitchen table was a sense of hope. I wasn't thinking about mountains or any landscape in particular, it was more about capturing an emotion. It was only after when they were drying that my husband said, they look like mountains at sunrise. 

Which one speaks to you?

Details:

  • Painting size approx 6" x 6" acrylic on board
  • Quality mount 12" x 12" with sturdy backing board
  • Individually signed and numbered 
  • Easy to frame (fits standard vinyl album sized frames)

This abstract landscape painting took on many forms over the months with each layer adding more texture and even more intrigue. I'm learning to take my time, to revisit paintings and work on them until they are ready.

I've done my best to take pictures but I can promise you that this piece looks much more interesting in real life. It currently hangs on my wall, partly to keep it from getting scuffed in my rack and partly because I love it. It speaks to me. 

It's called On The Edge because that's how I feel looking at it.

column3:
I loved the intuitive process of creating this abstract landscape painting (alongside it's sister painting ‘From The Lookout I'.) By layering colours and textures, then removing elements to reveal underlying marks, I allowed the painting to evolve organically into something unexpected and beautiful. I started off with a horizon line and then got to work with the blues. Before long it became clear it was to become a hazy, balmy seascape.

column3:
Painted on the autumn equinox, this evocative watercolour is a twelve-square tapestry of autumnal hues. Inspired by a rainy walk spent collecting leaves and conkers, each square showcases a unique blend of berry, orange, and gold tones. Together, they create a vibrant and harmonious celebration of the season's transformation and the burst of colour before winter's embrace.

This intricate patchwork design is perfect for adding a touch of autumnal warmth and sophistication to your home or office. I also painted Blackberry Crumble on the same day. 

Watercolour on watercolour paper. Framed

Original art. One only.

A4 size (210mm x 297mm). Framed.

column3:
I'm fascinated by the Fibonacci sequence, a mathematical pattern that appears in nature from seashells to galaxies and this intrigue has inspired me to create this art print.

The Fibonacci sequence is found in the arrangement of leaves on a plant, the spirals of a pine cone, and even the breeding patterns of rabbits. Its mathematical properties and its prevalence in the natural world have made it a subject of fascination for centuries.

I regularly use the Fibonacci sequence to guide my compositions to help me create aesthetically pleasing arrangements in my artwork. This print is a perfect gift for math enthusiasts, nature lovers, or anyone who appreciates the intersection of art and science.

column3:
This purrfffect art print features a curious black cat, beautifully illustrating one of the most intriguing paradoxes in quantum mechanics. Designed to spark thought and conversation, it's a must-have for science enthusiasts, cat lovers, or anyone fascinated by the mysteries of the universe.

Before hanging it on your wall, you could re-familiarise yourself with Schrödinger's famous cat paradox, which explores the concept of superposition and the role of observation in determining reality.

While you may not be able to explain the intricacies of quantum physics to every visitor, a basic understanding will help you navigate any curious questions. And if you happen to have a Sheldon Cooper-like guest, be prepared for an in-depth discussion over a warm beverage. String theory anyone?

column2: Jam First | Cream Tea | Art Print
column3:
The great scone debate: jam or cream first? It has to be jam first right? A thin layer of jam makes a great foundation to heap on the clotted cream. This is known in the UK as the Cornish way or as I like to say, the correct way.

However for those of you who prefer cream first - the Devon way (or the wrong way) then I've got you covered too. Just search Cream First in my shop.

  • Versatile and Stylish: Perfect for any occasion, this tote bag adds a stylish touch to your outfit. Use it as your go-to shopping bag, a trendy beach tote, or a chic carry-all for your daily errands.

column2: Blackbird Art Print - Listen For Me In The Music
column3:
This stained glass inspired blackbird art print is designed to offer solace and beauty during a time of loss. The blackbird, a symbol of resilience and new beginnings, perched gracefully amidst a vibrant carpet of bluebells evokes a sense of tranquility. This artwork is a heartfelt reminder that even in grief, there is beauty to be found. The blackbird, a harbinger of spring, whispers a message of hope and renewal, suggesting that the spirit of the departed may still be felt in the music that surrounds us. 

Inspired by the loving memory of Lynette Beard.

column2: HMS Prince Of Wales, Royal Navy Art Print
column3:
I'm very proud to say that this artwork is currently on aboard the HMS Prince of Wales. It was created as a companion piece to the Concorde Supersonic art print which was purchased to bring a bit of colour to the mess room.

The HMS Prince of Wales is a modern marvel of British engineering. As the second aircraft carrier in the Queen Elizabeth class, it's one of the largest warships ever built for the Royal Navy. Unlike traditional carriers, it uses a special design to operate STOVL (Short Take Off and Vertical Landing) aircraft. This allows the Prince of Wales to carry a powerful air group of F-35B fighters and Merlin helicopters for various tasks, making it a vital asset for the Royal Navy's global deployments.

column2: Mach Loop Wales - Aviation Travel Poster
column3:
This one is for all you aviation fans. Bring the colour and energy of the Mach Loop to your home with this dynamic print. Graphic, bold and bright this Mach Loop print becomes more than just a piece of art; it's a constant source of inspiration, sparking conversations and fueling the passion for aviation each time it catches the eye. It's an elegant reminder of the incredible feats of engineering and skill that define the world of aviation, making it an ideal choice for any enthusiast's wall display.

What is the Mach Loop?

The Mach Loop lies within the scenic landscapes of Snowdonia, Wales. It is nestled in the valleys between Dolgellau and Machynlleth and is well known for its low-level flying military aircraft, particularly fighter jets and reconnaissance planes. 

It offers a thrilling spectacle as aircraft roar through the picturesque Welsh valleys, providing an exhilarating experience. It has become a magnet for aviation aficionados, professional photographers, and thrill-seekers who appreciate the distinct roar of jet engines echoing through the hills and are able to negotiate the hilly terrain and welsh weather.

The planes manoeuvring at high speeds through the Mach Loop has become a cherished hotspot for those of us fascinated by the ever changing world of military aviation. It has earned a reputation as a prime destination and rite of passage for aviation enthusiasts, photographers and filmmakers.

I create all the artwork myself. From initial idea and sketch to working up the design through to printing and packing and finally posting out to you.

  • Printed on 230gsm matt paper
  • Archival quality pigment inks
  • Hand signed
  • Carefully packaged
  • Posted Royal Mail 24hrs tracked

column3:
Peace Poppies

Watercolour

A4 on watercolour board

I have created 10 watercolour paintings called 'Peace Poppies'. They are individually numbered and come in a range of colours. It will be a lucky dip selection, you won't know which original artwork you get until it lands on your doormat.

What you will know though, is that you are one of 10 kind people helping me raise funds for Medecins Sans Frontier.

We provide medical assistance to people affected by conflict, epidemics, disasters, or exclusion from healthcare. Our teams are made up of tens of thousands of health professionals, logistic and administrative staff - most of them hired locally. Our actions are guided by medical ethics and the principles of impartiality, independence and neutrality.

ALL of your £25 will go directly to MSF. I do not take anything from this. My aim is to turn 10 blank sheets of watercolour card into £250 that can be donated this week. Thank you SO much.

column2: Sunset On St Mary's - Isle Of Scilly
column3:
The Isle of Scilly. One of my favourite places in the world. In 2019 just before the world went bonkers, we stayed on the main island of St Marys and visited the others islands by boat. It was the best holiday ever and I can't wait to go back. This is the view of the harbour where the Scillonian docks. Bringing brave visitors across the choppy waters. The Scillonian is a flat bottomed boat allowing it to navigate the harbours at both high and low tides. However this also means it's at the mercy of the waves and on our ride over we were surrounded by green faced passengers being sick. But don't let that put you off. After five minutes of stepping on dry land you'll have forgotten all about the crossing. And you can always stump up the extra for the plane or the helicopter if you're feeling flushed.

column3:
We were on holiday in Cornwall and we went on a last minute trip to Bodmin Moor. We hadn't checked the weather forecast but was told that on a 'good day' you could see both the north and the south Cornish coast at the top of Roughtor. Well, you guessed it, it was not a 'good day' weather wise and on our climb to the top we could barely see ourselves let alone a coastline. Still, it was one of our most memorable days on holiday and we can't wait to go back. In this painting I have tried to capture the drama and emotion of a storm over the moors.

column2: You can Glo your own way - Glo Friends / Glo Worm Art Print
column3:
I love Glo Friends, they were THE toy of my childhood and I collected as many as I could. I loved everything about them. I cherished the packaging and I cut out the images on the boxes and blue tacked them around my room. My collection was given away to a relative when I was deemed past the age of toys (no such thing). So as an adult I bought them all back on Ebay and today I have a lovely little collection of Glow Friends above my desk.

Even if you are not familiar with the Playskool 1980's toy yourself you will no doubt guess that their most unique selling point was their ability to glow. Mine still work to this day, no batteries required, just put them under your bedside lamp for a few minutes and you'll have a glowing buddy to send you off to sleep.

These retro toys were sometimes known as Glo Worms as well as Glo Friends. There were soft body ones too that would sing a little lullaby but I didn't have one of those. Mine were all small hardbodied plastic ones and each had their own name and came with a cute little sleeping back with a button hole at the top which I never knew what it was for.

So if you like Glo Friends or Glo Worms then you might like this print. You can also check out my Glo Friends T-shirts too available through Teemill.

column2: Kellerman's Dirty Dancing Travel Art Print
column3:
Hey Baby, this is a piece of art you won't want to put in the corner! Escape to Kellerman's Mountain House in the Catskills and feast your Hungry Eyes on this print it really is Some Kind Of Beautiful. I've Had The Time Of My Life designing this, I'm not sure why it's taken me so long to think about doing a travel print for Kellerman's, but now I have it's here to Stay. That's enough Dirty Dancing puns from me, I don't want to Overload you.

I really did enjoy designing this Dirty Dancing print. I applied my usual bold and graphic style and I used a film still for the colour scheme (I'm calling the colour-way 'watermelon' obviously!) The still was from the final scene where the lights really made Baby's dress look pink and the darks are from Johnny's trousers. I spent hours finding the colours in those :)

Finally, for all you Dirty Dancing fans out there, I will leave you with the words of the legendary Max Kellerman:

"Join hands and hearts and voices
Voices, hearts and hands
At Kellerman's the friendships last long
As the mountains stand"

column2: Emley Moor Mast and Temporary Mast - Night Travel Poster
column3:
Now with added temporary mast!

This is a night time version of the first travel poster that I ever created. It was originally designed for my sister-in-law. She is a lovely Yorkshire lady and has always treasured this local landmark. She wanted some artwork that would look modern, fresh and eye-catching in her front room and at the same time evoke a sense of nostalgia for her childhood. It's such a much loved and iconic structure and can be seen for miles and miles around the county. I've had a great response to this Emley Moor Mast travel poster and I've been overwhelmed with how popular it has become.

I loved drawing this one, especially as I have lovely memories of visiting my friend Philippa there.

column3: The Harrier, informally referred to as the Harrier Jump Jet, is a family of jet-powered attack aircraft capable of vertical/short takeoff and landing operations (V/STOL). It was originally developed by British manufacturer Hawker Siddeley in the 1960s. The Harrier emerged as the only truly successful V/STOL design of the many attempted during that era. It was conceived to operate from improvised bases, such as car parks or forest clearings, without requiring large and vulnerable air bases. Later, the design was adapted for use from aircraft carriers.

10" x 8" (254mm x 203mm)

column3:
Original oil painting.

12" x 10" (305mm x 254mm)

My thoughts on this work: 

  • Impressionist brush strokes show the low dappled September light through the trees.
  • The tree tunnel (and the light at the end of it) as a metaphor for hope.
  • Best  enjoyed at a distance but close up is interesting and expressive.
  • Delicate and harmonious colours work together to create a sense of calm.
  • Colours used: earthy greens, pale blues, flecks of autumnal orange and mauve, vivid yellow highlights.

column2: Aviation Art Print - The Aircraft Collection
column3: Twenty of the most iconic planes brought to life in glorious technicolour. Using shadow and highlight I've tried to show off the best bits of each aircraft. This was quite the labour of love and took a while to get everything looking as sharp as I wanted, simple doesn't mean quick as you know. I hope any aviation fans out there enjoy the effort and detail that has gone into this artwork and if you do decide to purchase, then may it fill your home with colour. Planes featured: L - R: Spitfire • B2 Spirit • Red Arrows • English Electric Lightning • Concorde • Lancaster • Wright Flyer • Sopwith Camel • Harrier • Lysander • Vulcan • Catalina • Hercules • Mosquito • SR-71 Blackbird • F15 • Globemaster • F22 • Eurofighter • 747

column3:
Stevenage was the first New Town to be designated as such, in 1946. Its new town centre (built in the 1950s) included the first pedestrian-only shopping area in England. The iconic Grade II listed clock tower, designed by Leonard Vincent, one of the planners of the town centre, forms the focal point of the Town Square.

This is the landscape version of the original Stevenage print. This artwork is used on a a road sign welcoming all to the town.

column2: 'Let Them' Art Print | The Let Them Theory
column3:
Remind yourself daily of the 'Let Them' theory. Some people are getting tattoos saying 'Let Them' but if you want a less painful option then this art print is for you. I've illustrated the quote with a chessboard and used the Queen as she is the most powerful piece on the board. She can go wherever she wants, as far as she wants and in any direction. The Queen here represents the second part of the theory which is 'Let Me.

The Let Them Theory by international bestselling author Mel Robbins has become a worldwide phenomenon and is helping people to let go of things they can't change and concentrate on what they can do. If you are here reading this then you have probably read the book, but in case you haven't, I highly recommend it. It will add so much value to your life and give you so much brain space back.

The print comes with a tiny 3mm border which you can hide under any mount/matte or frame.

column3:
The Supermarine Spitfire is one of Britain’s most iconic pieces of engineering. Along with the Hawker Hurricane it was instrumental in battling the Luftwaffe, which gradually turned the tide of the second world war in favour of the allied forces. Based on a pre-war racing plane, it’s beautiful outline and the roar of it’s Rolls Royce Merlin engine are still a thrill to see and hear in the skies on a sunny day.

My husband told me I had to include this song by one of his favorite bands:

column3:
With her sleek silhouette and supersonic capabilities, the Concorde has a special place in the hearts of many. A select group have travelled on her, even fewer have piloted her but many of us over a certain age will remember the awe inspiring view as Concorde flew over. My earliest memory was the noise when she flew past at the airshows my dad used to take us too. In later years my husband and I lived in Chiswick (under the flightpath) and we would watch her cruise by from the comfort of our bed. "Did the earth move?", " No it was just the sonic boom".

This print is made up of 18 colourful illustrations that I hope captures the spirit of the beautiful Concorde.

column3: I created this piece on a day that I was feeling particularly frustrated at the world. Climate change, domestic politics and a feeling that a big wake up call is upon us. I love eastern European and Russian matchbox art so I took a lot of influence from that when designing this. I started off with a basic Pierrot figure and then it all went from there. The Time Is Now is a boldly coloured, surrealist art print which will probably attract a few comments from people visiting your house thinking that you've finally lost the plot - especially when they see that the eyes are actually a world map.

column2: Theatre - All The World's A Stage Poster
column3: This print was created as part of an exhibition run by The Circus Of Illustration and displayed in the Quarry Theatre - Bedford, during BedFringe festival. The brief was to capture the essence of the title 'All The World's A Stage'. I chose to focus on the small details that give people a sense of place. Classical sheet music and Shakespeare scripts; make up lights and a circus top hat; the fold up chairs in red moquette and the green glow of the exit lights. I hope you enjoy looking at the details in the print and that they remind you of all things theatrical.

column3:
Calling all NFL die-hards. Show your love for the game with this vibrant art print.

Packed with iconic imagery and NFL team colors, this piece isn't just wall art – it's a statement. It proudly declares your fandom to everyone who steps into your domain.

Sure, catching every game live can be tough across the pond, but that doesn't stop our family from soaking up the excitement of American Football. We truly embrace the "Any Given Sunday" spirit - after all, anything can happen on game day.

This bold and graphic print captures the essence of that thrilling uncertainty. It features every NFL team's colors, meticulously arranged by conference and division. Grids, colors, order – what's not to love?

And because quality matters, I use only the best:

    • Top-notch 300gsm matte paper for a premium feel.

    • High-quality pigment inks that resist fading, keeping your print vibrant for years to come.

    • 100% designed by me, from the initial sketch to the final delivery to your doorstep.

Bring the energy of the NFL into your home! Order your "Any Given Sunday" art print today!

column2: Emley Moor Mast Night Travel Poster
column3: This is a night time version of the first travel poster that I ever created. It was originally designed for my sister-in-law. She is a lovely Yorkshire lady and has always treasured this local landmark. She wanted some artwork that would look modern, fresh and eye-catching in her front room and at the same time evoke a sense of nostalgia for her childhood. It's such a much loved and iconic structure and can be seen for miles and miles around the county. I've had a great response to this Emley Moor Mast travel poster and I've been overwhelmed with how popular it has become.

column3: Mount Snowdon in the heart of Snowdonia in Wales. This travel poster is based on a photograph I took from the Miners track when myself and my family attempted to climb Snowdon in the summer holidays. column3:
This travel poster of Beachley Barracks was initially created as a one off commission as a surprise gift for someone who trained there. The gift was really well received and I was so pleased. Since then I've had quite a bit of interest in the print from others who have fond memories of training there. The original customer has kindly agreed for the print to go on general sale now so that more people can share in the memories.

I particularly enjoyed drawing the letter box. Days long before mobile phones this would have been the way people kept in touch with friends and family.

column3:
I initially designed this for myself, my walls needed cheering up one day and whilst I had a hundred other things I should have been doing, I did this instead. These are the things that were going through my head when choosing the colours:

  • January - Snowdrops; hazel catkins; cold skies; mudflats; winter pansy; cyclamen; house sparrows
  • February - Crocus; hyacinth; snow; daffodil shoots; leadened skies; muscari; buzzards
  • March - Daffodils; fresia; new shoots; algae; frogs
  • April - Swallow; moss; tulips; rose; caterpillars
  • May - Iris; poppies; warmer skies; impatiens; bluebells; red kites; cherry blossom
  • June - Warm skies; sand dunes; mown lawns; butterflies; strawberries
  • July - Gorse; ladybirds; gerbera; buttercups; sea
  • August - Sun; wheat fields; agapanthus; bumble bees
  • September - Blackberries; autumn leaves; foxes; evergreens
  • October - Haws; rosehips; sunsets; conkers; hedgehogs;
  • November - Lichen; fallen leaves; fallow fields; deep skies; bluetits
  • December - Clear starry nights; dogwood; snow; primrose; robins; holly; ivy;

column3:
The Great North Run is the largest half marathon in the world. Starting in Newcastle Upon Tyne and finishing in South Shields, this famous half marathon is well loved for its early autumn sunshine, its crackling atmosphere and of course, the Red Arrows flypast. I had already drawn the London Marathon print when the GNR was suggested to me so I already had a tried and tested way of graphically representing a mass of diverse people running. Drawing the Tyne Bridge was a little tricky at first, lots of steel and shadows to deal with but I got there in the end.

My favourite bit do draw though was the Red Arrows. Their breath taking displays at airshows always leaves me a little overwhelmed and it was a pleasure to spend time working out how to draw these and how to best do the iconic red white and blue smoke trails.

column3: Every time I go to Cambridge I feel inspired. It is a beautiful city and I feel very happy there. With this travel poster I've tried to tap into the feeling of hope and possibility I feel when I visit Cambridge. I have kept it light and airy with the dappled sun shining on the yellow stone wall. column3: A print for all the Northern Soul fans out there. It took me a while to design this print and get the feel right, but that just meant the soundtrack in my studio was awesome for longer. Hopefully there is something in it for everyone; whether you were out dancing at all-nighters back in the day or you've found it more recently. And if you've not been to a Northern Soul night but you quite fancy it then search out your nearest night (you may need to travel) and I promise you won't stop dancing. KTF.

column3:
For the last few years I have watched Star Gazing Live where Brian, Dara and a whole host of other big brained super humans often broadcast from Jodrell Bank Observatory. Every year I have the same thoughts. Firstly, 'I wish I had concentrated more in science' and secondly, 'I really should do a print of that place, it's awesome'.

Well, the latter is now done. This is my take on Jodrell Bank featuring the impressive Lovell Telescope. I've gone with a 1950's feel to this print given that was the era the telescope was constructed. I remember recently being wowed by seeing Jupiter, Venus and Mars all shining together one evening so I've put those in the print as a little reminder. The eagle eyed amongst you may also spot the ISS whizzing across the sky.

I hope this print appeals to the guys and gals with a sense of wonder, a curious mind and to anyone else that wishes they had paid more attention at school.

column3: I guess the Dartford Crossing has a different meaning to people depending on which way they are heading. From the Monday morning blues driving to work, to that Friday feeling heading home. If it's the former though you may not want this print hanging on your wall. That said, the sight of a bridge in the distance always makes me feel hopeful. Especially when the sunlight reflects off it. It's as if an adventure is waiting for me. And I really like that feeling. Anyone that uses the crossing regularly knows it looks nothing like this. It's much more...grey. This is the Dartford Crossing with a touch of 1930's glamour.

column3: On the north east coast of Scotland lies a small natural rock formation named Bow Fiddle Rock due to its unique shape - like a violin bow. The rock is a popular home to nesting seabirds and is an easily recognisable landmark for those walking the rugged coastline. I've tried to capture the scene during a beautiful Scottish sunrise, with most of the rock being in silhouette and just a few facets reflecting the early morning light. I love the colours in this print, they really remind me of the original Art Deco palettes used in the early travel posters.

column3: The picturesque fishing town of Whitby sits on on the east coast of England in North Yorkshire. Famed for its gothic abbey, the 199 steps (from which this view is taken), whale bone arches and of course its legendary fish and chips. I've walked the 199 steps and sampled the fish and chips, both were worth the journey. Whitby also has a thriving art community and is the inspiration to many imaginative works. Here's my attempt at capturing this beautiful seaside port, with the full moon casting shadows on land and throwing its light upon the sea. I hope you enjoy it.

column3: This piece came together very quickly and was great fun to do.  I was supposed to be doing other things really and I wasn't due to start it until next week but I got the urge and I didn't stop until it was finished (about midnight). I started by trying to get the basic shape of the hill; I really wanted to nail the strange shaped top and Google Earth became my friend in getting the right angle. Next came the sunset and once I was happy with that, I knew what colours the landscape had to be. Of course I ramped the colours up a bit, after all this is art, however I am quietly confident that at one moment in the history of time Roseberry Topping (now owned by the National Trust) has been as colourful as this.

Built in 1844 this well loved folly is officially known as The Earl of Durham's Monument but known locally as Penshaw Monument. It is a replica of the Temple of Hephaestus in Athens and also features on the badge of Sunderland Football Club. A real local treasure.

column3:
There's something special about Brancaster beach. Everyone that goes there feels like it's their own little secret getaway. For us as a family it's wonderful. Firstly, we can get there and back in a day which is great for us landlocked Bedfordians. Secondly the beach is huge, massive in fact. When the tide is out you need to factor in a good 15 mins walk (with little kids) just to have a paddle. When the tide is in however, you've got one eye on the car park willing it not to flood.

There are no fairground rides, no arcade machines polluting the airwaves, just birdsong, the sound of the wind whipping over the sand dunes and the waves crashing. It really is the perfect escape. If you've been there yourself then you'll understand just how special it is, and if you haven't, get planning your trip, you can thank me later.

column3:
Starting at the Verrazano-Narrows bridge on Staten Island, the New York Marathon winds its way through Brooklyn, Queens, skimming the Bronx and into Manhattan. What a route.

I’ve tried to capture the energy at the start of the race – people from all nations packed onto the bridge, full of adrenaline and hope – oblivious to the lactic acid build up awaiting them on Fifth Avenue. I originally started this illustration with a different angle in mind (looking north over the bridge from ground level) but after a few hours work I realised that at this angle this bridge could be anywhere! What I needed was Manhattan in the distance. Obvious really, and the artwork certainly did take on a new energy once I'd cottoned on to this. I really loved doing the city skyline, but was deeply moved not to be doing the Twin Towers.

I've never been to New York but I hope to visit one day with my family. It will not be via the NYC marathon though, I do not cultivate that kind of stamina. Huge kudos to anyone who has run it though. Huge.

column3:
The iconic split screen campervan. A universal symbol of the free spirit, of bacon butties, campfires, and sunsets. And rain. What more is there to say. I simply love them, in fact I used to own one (a T25 called Boris). I'm now saving up for a more reliable camper.

This was quite a tricky one for me to get right due to the extremely low angle I took. I had a very clear idea in my head but I just couldn't get it out on paper. In fact I had to shelve it for a few months and focus on other work as I struggled so much. My perseverance paid off in the end though and I'm super pleased with it. I plan to do the T2, T25, T4 and T5 at some point too. And the beetle, I can't forget the beetle.

column3:
My brother and his family live near Denby Dale and it is such a beautiful part of the country. I love the contrast between the stone walls and the beautiful countryside. The viaduct crossing this picturesque village is a breathtaking sight and one the locals are rightly proud of.

Personally I love this view because it means I’m spending time with my family. This poster was suggested by the Denby Dale Tea Rooms. They currently display and sell my Emley Moor and Castle Hill posters and felt that a Denby Dale one would go down well too. It’s lovely for me to receive suggestions from people who are proud of their home towns and their continued support is much appreciated.

There are many views of the viaduct but this was my favourite. By offering a glimpse of the town in the background you get a sense of scale and contrast. It wasn’t the easiest view to tackle and there would have been easier options, but for me this was the view that makes the poster unmistakably Denby Dale.

column3:
I’ve wanted to do a poster of the Cardington sheds for ages. I started it months ago, but I struggled with the angles and then other deadlines got in the way. But I’ve gone back, I’ve stuck with it and it’s finally finished and I’m super pleased. I really love the sheds so this was a special one for me to work on  –  they are local to me so I shall be hanging this one on my wall.

For those who aren’t familiar with them a bit of background info: these imposing airship hangers nestled in the Bedfordshire countryside can be seen for miles around and are well respected and loved locally. Known as ‘sheds’ these enormous structures have had a varied and glamorous history since the beginning of their construction in 1915. Initially created to build rigid airships the site soon became a Royal Air Force station and it was during this era that the famous R101 was built.

More recently shed 2 has been given a new lease of life as a film studio. Although it’s not soundproof, the shed offers a versatile space which rivals any Hollywood studio for size. Films such as Batman, Pan, Inception and Harry Potter have all made use of these vast buildings. And yes I’ve driven past them hoping for a glimpse of Hugh Jackman but alas I don’t think he got the memo that day. Shame.

column3:
Castle Hill is a much loved and well known landmark to those living in West Yorkshire. The hilltop has a long and mysterious history and evidence shows that humans have called this area home for over 4000 years.

Sitting proudly on top of the shale and sandstone hill is the Grade II listed Victoria Tower which can be seen from many miles away. Surprisingly the tower is relatively new and was built in 1897 to mark Queen Victoria’s exceptionally long reign.

However the hilltop is littered with remains of banks and ditches dating back from the early Iron Age. It seems like every generation has left its mark on this beautiful area. Today even the overhead electricity cables have been buried to allow for kite flying.

column3:
This iconic view is a special place for many people, but for one of my dear friends it has extra meaning. At 5am on the day of their wedding, my friend and her husband-to-be walked to the top of this beautiful hill top. Together they looked out at this exact view and contemplated all the great things that lay ahead of them as a married couple. When I started doing these posters my friend asked if I could do one of Orrest Head – and so here it is. I’ve been there myself too and it really is this stunning. If you’ve not been I urge you to go, it won’t disappoint.

This poster came together really well. Sometimes things just flow and this print is an example of that for me. It was a pleasure to do. In fact I didn’t rework any part of it, it just sort of happened in the right order. Those projects are the best ones for me. The one where your years of practice (whatever your trade) just kick in and you sort of go on auto-pilot and end up achieving something cool and thinking wow how did I just do that?

column3: This poster was suggested by a good friend of mine. Her son is lucky enough to have a view of Alexandra Palace from his house and she wanted to surprise him with a unique birthday present. Take a look at the detail in the window - that took me as long to create as the rest of the building.

column3: This poster was created to sit next to the Emley Moor Mast print. I created this for my brother, a wonderful man who has great memories of his travels in Australia. He told me of this perfect day in Port Macquarie. A day when everything seemed to fit into place. The way the light fell, the food, the company, the dolphins showing off, it all came together to create this lasting memory which I've tried to capture for him.
